collection: Nonfiction, Biography, History, Science, Poetry & Drama

authors.name: Yuval Noah Harari
authors.biography: Israeli historian whose sweeping accounts of humanity's past and future made him one of the most read public intellectuals alive.

books.title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
books.description: From the cognitive revolution to credit cards, a provocative tour of how one ape species came to rule the planet through shared fictions.

books.title: Homo Deus: A Brief History of Tomorrow
books.description: Having beaten famine, plague, and war, humanity turns to immortality and godhood — and may engineer itself out of relevance.

authors.name: Bill Bryson
authors.biography: Iowa-born Anglophile whose travelogues and popular science books make erudition feel like good company.

books.title: A Short History of Nearly Everything
books.description: How do we know what we know? Bryson tours the whole of science — atoms to asteroids — through the eccentric people who figured it out.

books.title: A Walk in the Woods
books.description: Bryson and his hopelessly unfit friend Katz attempt the Appalachian Trail, with bears, blisters, and the funniest trail companionship ever recorded.

books.title: Notes from a Small Island
books.description: Before moving back to America, Bryson takes a valedictory ramble around Britain, the country that taught him tea, hedgerows, and queueing.

authors.name: Jon Krakauer
authors.biography: American mountaineer-journalist whose immersive reporting probes obsession, wilderness, and belief at their most lethal.

books.title: Into the Wild
books.description: Chris McCandless gave away his savings and walked into the Alaskan bush; Krakauer retraces the idealism and miscalculation that ended in an abandoned bus.

books.title: Into Thin Air
books.description: Krakauer's firsthand account of the 1996 Everest disaster, when a storm caught four expeditions high on the mountain and eight climbers died.

books.title: Under the Banner of Heaven
books.description: A 1984 double murder committed on God's orders opens an investigation into Mormon fundamentalism and the violence of certainty.

authors.name: Erik Larson
authors.biography: American master of narrative history who builds page-turners entirely from archival fact.

books.title: The Devil in the White City
books.description: The architect of the 1893 Chicago World's Fair and the serial killer H.H. Holmes, who built a murder hotel beside it, share one gilded stage.

books.title: Dead Wake
books.description: The last crossing of the Lusitania, told hour by hour from the liner's decks and the U-boat's periscope.

books.title: The Splendid and the Vile
books.description: Churchill's first year as PM — the Blitz, the speeches, the family dramas — drawn from diaries of those inside his household.

authors.name: Daniel Kahneman
authors.biography: Israeli-American psychologist who won the Nobel in economics for showing how predictably irrational human judgment is.

books.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
books.description: Two systems run your mind — fast intuition and slow reasoning — and knowing how they fail explains everything from bad bets to overconfident experts.

authors.name: Malcolm Gladwell
authors.biography: Canadian journalist and podcaster who turned social science storytelling into a publishing genre of its own.

books.title: Outliers
books.description: Success is less about talent than timing, culture, and ten thousand hours — why Bill Gates, the Beatles, and Korean pilots are the stories they are.

books.title: The Tipping Point
books.description: How ideas, products, and behaviors spread like epidemics, tipped by connectors, mavens, and salesmen.

authors.name: James Clear
authors.biography: American writer on habits and decision-making whose first book became a perennial number-one bestseller.

books.title: Atomic Habits
books.description: Tiny changes compound: a practical system for building good habits and breaking bad ones through identity, environment, and one percent improvements.

authors.name: Cal Newport
authors.biography: Georgetown computer scientist arguing for concentration and craftsmanship in an economy of distraction.

books.title: Deep Work
books.description: The ability to focus without distraction is the superpower of the knowledge economy — and a set of rules for cultivating it.

books.title: Digital Minimalism
books.description: A philosophy of technology use: a thirty-day declutter, then reintroduce only the tools that serve what you deeply value.

authors.name: Brené Brown
authors.biography: Texas social work researcher whose studies of shame and vulnerability leapt from academia to global stages.

books.title: Daring Greatly
books.description: Vulnerability is not weakness but the birthplace of courage, connection, and creativity — research-backed permission to be seen.

books.title: Atlas of the Heart
books.description: A map of eighty-seven emotions and experiences, naming the difference between envy and jealousy, shame and guilt, so we can speak our inner lives.

authors.name: Tara Westover
authors.biography: American historian raised by survivalists in the Idaho mountains who first set foot in a classroom at seventeen.

books.title: Educated
books.description: No birth certificate, no school, a junkyard childhood — and the will that carried Westover from her father's mountain to a Cambridge PhD, at the cost of her family.

authors.name: Michelle Obama
authors.biography: Lawyer, public health advocate, and First Lady of the United States from 2009 to 2017.

books.title: Becoming
books.description: From Chicago's South Side to the White House, an intimate account of marriage, motherhood, identity, and eight years under history's brightest lights.

authors.name: Trevor Noah
authors.biography: South African comedian and former Daily Show host, born to a union that apartheid law made criminal.

books.title: Born a Crime
books.description: Stories of a mixed-race boyhood in apartheid South Africa, anchored by a fearless, devout mother who threw him from a moving minibus to save his life.

authors.name: Nelson Mandela
authors.biography: Anti-apartheid revolutionary who emerged from twenty-seven years in prison to become South Africa's first democratically elected president.

books.title: Long Walk to Freedom
books.description: Mandela's own account of the struggle: rural boyhood, underground resistance, Robben Island, and the negotiated miracle of a free South Africa.

authors.name: Anne Frank
authors.biography: German-born Jewish diarist who chronicled two years in hiding in Amsterdam before dying at Bergen-Belsen at fifteen.

books.title: The Diary of a Young Girl
books.description: From a secret annex above her father's office, a teenage girl records fear, hope, first love, and an undimmed belief in people, in the most read document of the Holocaust.

authors.name: Elie Wiesel
authors.biography: Romanian-born survivor of Auschwitz and Buchenwald, Nobel Peace laureate, and lifelong witness against forgetting.

books.title: Night
books.description: A fifteen-year-old's deportation from Sighet to Auschwitz, where faith, family, and childhood are consumed — testimony pared to its unbearable essentials.

authors.name: Laura Hillenbrand
authors.biography: American narrative historian who, writing through severe chronic illness, produced two of the most acclaimed nonfiction sagas of her era.

books.title: Unbroken
books.description: Olympic runner Louie Zamperini survives a plane crash, forty-seven days adrift, and Japanese POW camps in an epic of resilience.

books.title: Seabiscuit
books.description: An undersized horse, a half-blind jockey, and a silent trainer become Depression America's favorite underdog story.

authors.name: Rebecca Skloot
authors.biography: American science journalist who spent a decade reporting the human story behind biology's most famous cells.

books.title: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
books.description: Cells taken without consent from a Black tobacco farmer in 1951 became medicine's immortal HeLa line; her family learned decades later.

authors.name: Siddhartha Mukherjee
authors.biography: Indian-American oncologist whose 'biographies' of cancer and the gene won the Pulitzer and redefined popular medicine writing.

books.title: The Emperor of All Maladies
books.description: A 4,000-year biography of cancer — from Egyptian papyri to targeted therapy — told through patients, surgeons, and the war on a shape-shifting enemy.

books.title: The Gene: An Intimate History
books.description: From Mendel's peas to CRISPR, the story of heredity braided with the author's own family history of mental illness.

authors.name: Oliver Sacks
authors.biography: British neurologist whose compassionate case histories turned clinical strangeness into literature.

books.title: The Man Who Mistook His Wife for a Hat
books.description: Clinical tales of patients who lost faces, bodies, or decades — and what their conditions reveal about the self the brain constructs.

books.title: Musicophilia
books.description: Sudden pianists after lightning strikes, amusia, earworms: case studies of music's strange power over the human brain.

authors.name: Stephen Hawking
authors.biography: Cambridge cosmologist who probed black holes and the universe's origin while living five decades with ALS.

books.title: A Brief History of Time
books.description: From the Big Bang to black holes, the bestselling tour of space, time, and the search for a theory of everything.

authors.name: Carl Sagan
authors.biography: American astronomer and the twentieth century's great evangelist for the scientific worldview and the pale blue dot we share.

books.title: Cosmos
books.description: Fifteen billion years of cosmic evolution and the human story of discovering it, the companion to the most watched science series of its time.

books.title: The Demon-Haunted World
books.description: Science as a candle in the dark: a defense of skeptical thinking against pseudoscience, superstition, and the dumbing-down of public discourse.

books.title: Pale Blue Dot
books.description: Inspired by Voyager's last photograph of Earth, a vision of humanity's future among the planets.

authors.name: Richard Feynman
authors.biography: Nobel-winning physicist, bongo player, and safecracker whose curiosity made him science's most entertaining storyteller.

books.title: Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!
books.description: Adventures of a curious character: cracking Los Alamos safes, sketching in strip clubs, and out-thinking everyone while having more fun.

authors.name: Carlo Rovelli
authors.biography: Italian theoretical physicist and founder of loop quantum gravity who writes about time and space like a poet.

books.title: Seven Brief Lessons on Physics
books.description: Relativity, quanta, black holes, and our place in the cosmos in under a hundred luminous pages.

books.title: The Order of Time
books.description: Time passes faster in the mountains than at sea level, and 'now' means nothing across the universe — what physics actually says about time.

authors.name: Robert M. Sapolsky
authors.biography: Stanford neuroscientist and primatologist who spent decades with Kenyan baboons studying stress, hierarchy, and why we behave.

books.title: Behave
books.description: Why did that behavior happen? Sapolsky answers across timescales — one second before, in the womb, in evolutionary deep time — in a grand synthesis of human biology.

books.title: Why Zebras Don't Get Ulcers
books.description: Zebras sprint from lions and relax; humans marinate in chronic stress designed for emergencies — what that does to bodies, and what helps.

authors.name: Mary Roach
authors.biography: America's funniest science writer, who asks the questions about corpses, space toilets, and digestion that everyone else is too polite to ask.

books.title: Stiff: The Curious Lives of Human Cadavers
books.description: What happens to donated bodies: crash tests, surgical practice, body farms — a hilarious, respectful tour of mortality's practical side.

books.title: Packing for Mars
books.description: The strange science of keeping squishy, smelly, psychologically fragile humans alive in space, from zero-G toilets to year-long isolation tests.

authors.name: Walter Isaacson
authors.biography: American biographer of geniuses — from Franklin to Einstein to Jobs — and former editor of Time.

books.title: Steve Jobs
books.description: The authorized yet unsparing portrait of Apple's co-founder: visionary, tyrant, perfectionist, drawn from forty interviews with Jobs himself.

books.title: Einstein: His Life and Universe
books.description: How a rebellious patent clerk imagined riding a light beam and rewrote physics — and how fame, politics, and quantum doubt filled the rest of his life.

books.title: Leonardo da Vinci
books.description: From 7,200 notebook pages, the most relentlessly curious mind in history: anatomy, flying machines, and the smile of the Mona Lisa.

authors.name: Ron Chernow
authors.biography: American biographer of founders and financiers whose Hamilton biography inspired the musical.

books.title: Alexander Hamilton
books.description: The orphan immigrant who built America's financial system, fought its first media wars, and died on a Weehawken ledge.

books.title: Washington: A Life
books.description: The Pulitzer-winning portrait of the ambitious, passionate man behind the marble myth.

authors.name: David McCullough
authors.biography: Two-time Pulitzer-winning American historian whose warm narrative voice made the past a bestseller.

books.title: John Adams
books.description: The irascible, incorruptible second president and his half-century partnership with Abigail, in McCullough's Pulitzer-winning life.
One precarious year of the Revolution, when Washington's ragged army lost New York and saved the cause at Trenton.

authors.name: Barbara W. Tuchman
authors.biography: Two-time Pulitzer-winning historian who proved narrative history could be both rigorous and riveting.

books.title: The Guns of August
books.description: The first month of World War I, when rigid war plans and royal vanities marched Europe into catastrophe.

authors.name: Antony Beevor
authors.biography: Former British Army officer whose archival histories of WWII's great battles read with novelistic force.

books.title: Stalingrad
books.description: The pitiless five-month battle that broke the Wehrmacht, told from generals' maps and soldiers' letters on both sides of the Volga.

authors.name: Patrick Radden Keefe
authors.biography: New Yorker staff writer whose investigative epics read like thrillers and hold power precisely to account.

books.title: Say Nothing
books.description: A 1972 Belfast disappearance unspools into the whole tragedy of the Troubles, the IRA's secrets, and the price of silence.

books.title: Empire of Pain
books.description: Three generations of the Sackler family, from Valium marketing to OxyContin, and the dynasty behind America's opioid catastrophe.

authors.name: Michelle Zauner
authors.biography: Korean American musician (Japanese Breakfast) whose grief memoir about food, family, and identity became a phenomenon.

books.title: Crying in H Mart
books.description: Losing her Korean mother to cancer at twenty-five, Zauner cooks her way back to her — jjigae by jjigae — in a memoir of appetite and grief.

authors.name: Viktor E. Frankl
authors.biography: Viennese psychiatrist and Holocaust survivor who founded logotherapy on the conviction that meaning sustains life.

books.title: Man's Search for Meaning
books.description: A psychiatrist's testimony from Auschwitz and the therapy it forged: those who have a why to live can bear almost any how.

authors.name: Marcus Aurelius
authors.biography: Roman emperor from 161 to 180 CE whose private notebooks of Stoic self-instruction became one of philosophy's most consulted works. Lived 121–180 CE; exact dates predate the calendar fields used here.

books.title: Meditations
books.description: An emperor's private reminders on mortality, duty, and tranquility — Stoicism practiced at the height of absolute power. (Year reflects first printed edition.)

authors.name: Sun Tzu
authors.biography: Ancient Chinese strategist traditionally credited with the most influential treatise on conflict ever written. Lived c. 544–496 BCE; exact dates predate the calendar fields used here.

books.title: The Art of War
books.description: Thirteen chapters on winning without fighting, deception, and terrain, applied for centuries from battlefields to boardrooms. (Year reflects first Western edition.)

authors.name: Robert Greene
authors.biography: American author of amoral, historically armed handbooks on power, seduction, and mastery.

books.title: The 48 Laws of Power
books.description: Machiavelli for the modern cubicle: forty-eight laws of influence drawn from courtiers, con artists, and kings.

authors.name: Stephen R. Covey
authors.biography: American leadership educator whose principle-centered framework became the canonical business self-help book.

books.title: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
books.description: Begin with the end in mind, seek first to understand: a character-based architecture for personal and professional effectiveness.

authors.name: Dale Carnegie
authors.biography: Missouri farm boy turned public-speaking teacher whose 1936 course on human relations never left print.

books.title: How to Win Friends and Influence People
books.description: Remember names, avoid arguments, give honest appreciation: the original handbook of getting along, still selling by the millions.

authors.name: Eric Ries
authors.biography: Silicon Valley entrepreneur whose build-measure-learn loop became the operating manual for modern startups.

books.title: The Lean Startup
books.description: Treat a startup as a series of experiments: minimum viable products, validated learning, and the discipline to pivot or persevere.

authors.name: Daniel H. Pink
authors.biography: American author on work and behavior, former White House speechwriter, translator of research into management practice.

books.title: Drive
books.description: Carrots and sticks backfire for creative work; autonomy, mastery, and purpose are what actually motivate.

authors.name: Michael Lewis
authors.biography: Former bond salesman who became the great explainer of money's absurdities through irresistible characters.

books.title: The Big Short
books.description: The misfits who saw the subprime collapse coming and bet against the global financial system — and won as it burned.

books.title: Moneyball
books.description: How the broke Oakland A's used undervalued statistics to beat teams spending three times more, and started a revolution beyond baseball.

authors.name: Morgan Housel
authors.biography: American financial writer whose essays on money psychology became one of the bestselling finance books ever.

books.title: The Psychology of Money
books.description: Nineteen short lessons on why doing well with money has little to do with intelligence and everything to do with behavior.

authors.name: William Shakespeare
authors.biography: Stratford glover's son whose thirty-eight plays and 154 sonnets remain the centerpiece of English literature and the world's stages.

books.title: Hamlet
books.description: The Prince of Denmark, commanded by his father's ghost to revenge, thinks his way through madness, mortality, and the readiness that is all.

books.title: Macbeth
books.description: Three witches promise a Scottish thane the crown, and ambition does the rest, in the bloodiest and swiftest of the tragedies.

books.title: Romeo and Juliet
books.description: Two teenagers from feuding Verona houses marry in secret and die in error, in the defining tragedy of young love.

books.title: A Midsummer Night's Dream
books.description: Four lovers, a troupe of amateur actors, and a quarreling fairy court tangle for one enchanted night in the woods outside Athens.

authors.name: Homer
authors.biography: The traditional poet of archaic Greece credited with the two epics at the foundation of Western literature. Flourished c. 8th century BCE; exact dates predate the calendar fields used here.

books.title: The Iliad
books.description: The wrath of Achilles in the tenth year of the Trojan War: honor, grief, and the terrible glory of battle. (Year reflects Chapman's first English edition.)

books.title: The Odyssey
books.description: Ten years after Troy, Odysseus contends with cyclopes, sorceresses, and the sea god's grudge to reach Ithaca and the wife who waits. (Year reflects Chapman's English edition.)

authors.name: Walt Whitman
authors.biography: Brooklyn printer and Civil War nurse whose free-verse celebration of the democratic self founded American poetry.

books.title: Leaves of Grass
books.description: 'I am large, I contain multitudes': Whitman's lifelong, ever-expanding song of the body, the road, and America itself.

authors.name: Emily Dickinson
authors.biography: The recluse of Amherst, who published almost nothing in life and left nearly eighteen hundred poems that remade the art.

books.title: The Complete Poems of Emily Dickinson
books.description: Death, bees, eternity, and dashes: the full restored poems of America's most original poet, as she actually wrote them.

authors.name: Robert Frost
authors.biography: Four-time Pulitzer-winning poet of New England's stone walls and dark woods, deceptively plainspoken and endlessly quoted.

books.title: The Poetry of Robert Frost
books.description: The collected poems: roads not taken, woods on snowy evenings, and fences that may or may not make good neighbors.

authors.name: Pablo Neruda
authors.biography: Chilean poet, diplomat, and Nobel laureate whose odes and love songs made him the most read poet of the twentieth century.

books.title: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair
books.description: Written at nineteen, the sensuous, melancholy cycle that made Neruda the voice of love in Spanish.

authors.name: Mary Oliver
authors.biography: Pulitzer-winning American poet of attention, whose walks in the Provincetown woods produced the era's most shared lines about how to live.

books.title: Devotions
books.description: Oliver's own selection from five decades: grasshoppers, wild geese, and the one wild and precious life her readers keep asking about.

authors.name: Amanda Gorman
authors.biography: Youngest inaugural poet in U.S. history, whose 2021 recitation made poetry a live national event.

books.title: The Hill We Climb
books.description: The inaugural poem of January 2021 in a standalone edition: a country bruised but whole, and the dawn that blooms as we free it.

authors.name: Lorraine Hansberry
authors.biography: First Black woman to have a play produced on Broadway, dead at thirty-four, whose work still anchors the American stage.

books.title: A Raisin in the Sun
books.description: An insurance check could move the Younger family out of their Chicago kitchenette — if they can agree on a dream deferred no longer.

authors.name: Arthur Miller
authors.biography: American playwright who put the common man at the center of tragedy and the McCarthy era on trial.

books.title: Death of a Salesman
books.description: Willy Loman, sixty-three and worn through, collides with the gap between the American Dream he sold and the life he lived.

books.title: The Crucible
books.description: The Salem witch trials as anatomy of hysteria: vengeance dressed as piety, and John Proctor's name as the last thing he owns.

authors.name: Tennessee Williams
authors.biography: Mississippi-born playwright of desire and its casualties, whose Southern poetics transformed Broadway.

books.title: A Streetcar Named Desire
books.description: Faded belle Blanche DuBois arrives at her sister's New Orleans flat, where Stanley Kowalski's brute reality awaits her illusions.

authors.name: Maya Angelou
authors.biography: Poet, memoirist, and civil rights worker whose account of a Black girlhood in the Jim Crow South opened American autobiography.

books.title: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
books.description: From Stamps, Arkansas, to San Francisco, the first volume of Angelou's autobiography: trauma, silence, and the voice that broke it.

books.title: And Still I Rise
books.description: Angelou's third collection, anchored by the title anthem of defiance and dust that rises.
